column3: content
ICSs are the most effective medications for long-term control of persistent asthma.
ICSs reduce chronic airway inflammation by acting directly on the lungs.
ICSs should be taken daily to prevent asthma symptoms and attacks.
ICS dosing should be titrated to the minimum effective dose needed for control.
Mouth irritation and thrush are common side effects of ICSs.
Growth delay (approximately 1 cm) is a less common side effect of ICSs in children.
ICSs are generally safe for use in pregnant women when used to control asthma.
The benefits of ICSs outweigh the risks when used at the lowest effective dose.
Clinicians must monitor patient response and titrate ICS doses to the minimum effective dose.
Patient education on proper inhaler technique and adherence is required for ICSs.
ICSs are foundational for asthma control and should be used daily.
LABAs are add-on controllers used in combination with ICSs for asthma not controlled by low-dose ICS alone.
LABAs improve lung function and reduce asthma symptoms when combined with ICSs.
LABAs should only be used with ICSs and not for quick relief of acute symptoms.
Patients must be educated on daily ICS use even when on LABAs.
The maximum daily dose for salmeterol is ≤100 mcg/day.
The maximum daily dose for formoterol is ≤24 mcg/day.
LABAs should never be used alone for long-term control.
Black Box warning issued by FDA due to increased risk of severe asthma exacerbations with LABAs.
Benefits of LABAs must be weighed against the risk of severe exacerbations.
LABA use should not replace ICSs.
Patient education on avoiding LABA use in children under 4 years (unless approved for formoterol) is required.
Monitor growth
column5: and lung function to adjust ICS and LABA doses appropriately.
